Hi, you've reached Smith Plumbing! This is Sam, how can I help you today?
Oh hello dear. Yes, I'm calling because my water heater, it's... well, the water just doesn't seem to get as hot as it used to. Takes forever to warm up.
I see, so your water heater isn't getting as hot as it used to and it's taking longer to warm up. How old is the unit, do you know?
Oh goodness, let me think. We had it put in when... it must have been, oh, maybe 2010? 2011? My husband would have known, God rest him.
I'm sorry to hear that. So it's about 15 years old then. At that age, water heaters do start to lose efficiency.

Hi, you've reached Smith Plumbing! This is Sam, how can I help you today?
Oh hello dear. Yes, I'm calling because my water heater, it's... well, the water just doesn't seem to get as hot as it used to. Takes forever to warm up.
I see, so your water heater isn't getting as hot as it used to and it's taking longer to warm up. How old is the unit, do you know?
Oh goodness, let me think. We had it put in when... it must have been, oh, maybe 2010? 2011? My husband would have known, God rest him.
I'm sorry to hear that. So it's about 15 years old then. At that age, water heaters do start to lose efficiency.
Oh my. So does that mean I need a whole new one? That sounds expensive. I'm on a fixed income, you see.
Not necessarily! Sometimes it just needs the sediment flushed out or the heating element replaced, which is a lot less than a full replacement.

Hi, you've reached Smith Plumbing! This is Sam, how can I help you today?
Oh hello dear. Yes, I'm calling because my water heater, it's... well, the water just doesn't seem to get as hot as it used to. Takes forever to warm up.
I see, so your water heater isn't getting as hot as it used to and it's taking longer to warm up. How old is the unit, do you know?
Oh goodness, let me think. We had it put in when... it must have been, oh, maybe 2010? 2011? My husband would have known, God rest him.
I'm sorry to hear that. So it's about 15 years old then. At that age, water heaters do start to lose efficiency.
Oh my. So does that mean I need a whole new one? That sounds expensive. I'm on a fixed income, you see.
Not necessarily! Sometimes it just needs the sediment flushed out or the heating element replaced, which is a lot less than a full replacement.
Sediment, you said? Is that like... dirt inside the tank?
Exactly, minerals build up over time at the bottom of the tank and make it harder for the heater to do its job. A flush can really help.

The flapper? Is that something I'd need a plumber for?

Smith Plumbing, this is Lisa. What can I do for you today?
Hello, dear. I'm not sure who to call about this but... there's a funny smell in my house. It's been there since this morning, kind of like eggs?
Ma'am, a rotten egg smell — that could be a gas leak. I need you to leave the house right now and call 911 from outside. Don't turn on any lights or anything on the way out.
Oh my, really? I thought maybe something just went bad in the refrigerator. Are you sure it's that serious?
Yes ma'am, we always treat a rotten egg smell as gas until we know for sure. Please head outside right now — don't use the stove or flip any switches. Can you do that for me?
Okay, okay, let me get my coat... alright I'm walking to the front door now. Should I leave it open?
Yes, leave the door open for ventilation. Once you're outside, call 911 first — they'll send someone to check the gas. Then call us back and we'll send a plumber out too.
I'm outside now. Alright, I'll call 911. But can you still send someone? I don't really understand all this gas business.
Absolutely, we'll have someone come check your lines. Can I get your name and address, sweetheart?
It's Dorothy Harmon, I'm at 88 Elm Court. My number is 555-0234.
Okay Dorothy, so — call 911 first, tell them you smell gas at 88 Elm Court. We'll send our plumber out today to inspect your gas lines once the fire department clears you.

Smith Plumbing, this is Katie!

Hi Katie! So I have kind of a random question. My wife and I just bought this gorgeous new kitchen faucet from a home show — one of those fancy ones with the pull-down sprayer, you know? And I'm wondering if you guys install those or if that's more of a handyman thing.
Oh nice! Yeah, we absolutely install kitchen faucets — that's right up our alley.
Perfect! So here's my other question — we already have the faucet, it's sitting in the box in the garage. Do you guys charge less if we supply the parts? Or is it the same either way?
Great question — yes, since you've already got the faucet, you'd just be paying the labor, which is our $89 service call plus usually about $125 to $175 for the install depending on the setup.

Oh that's not bad at all! Honestly, I was expecting more. My buddy Steve paid like $400 at some other place but I think they ripped him off. Do you guys handle the old faucet removal too, or do I need to take that off first?
We handle everything — removal of the old one, installing the new one, hooking up the water lines, and making sure there are no leaks.
Awesome. And one more thing — the hot water supply valve under the sink is a little corroded. Would the plumber be able to look at that while they're there?
Absolutely, they can check that valve and replace it if needed — we'd just add the cost of the valve which is usually around $20 to $30.
